I had the wonderful opportunity to attend a batik craft cooperation event, and it was truly an eye-opening and enriching perience. From learning the history of batik to trying the actual wax-resist dyeing technique, every step felt deeply rooted in tradition and creativity.Working alongside skilled local artisans gave me a new appreciation for the effort, detail, and passion that goes into every piece. It was more than jt an art workshop—it was a connection to heritage, community, and stainable craftsmanship.What stood out most was the spirit of cooperation—how local communities, social enterprises, and participants came together to presee and promote this beautiful art form while also creating economic opportunities.I left the event not only with a beautiful, hand-dyed piece I made myself, but also with a renewed respect for Malaysia’s cultural richness. Highly recommended for anyone who values creativity with purpose.

A Cultural Gem in Kuantan – Hands-On Batik perience at Natural Batik VillageI visited Natural Batik Village located in Balok, Kuantan, Pahang, and it was a truly enriching and enjoyable perience!The location is spacio and wheelchair accessible, making it suitable for visitors of all ages and mobility levels. I had the chance to obsee how Pahang-style batik (block printing) is traditionally made. The staff were friendly and welcoming — they plained the batik-making process in detail, which made the perience even more meaningful.After that, I plored the retail section where they sell a wide variety of batik fabrics, as well as batik clothing for men, women, and children, in many beautiful designs and patterns. Pahang batik has its own unique style, distinctly different from the batik found in Kelantan or Terengganu, which makes it special and worth discovering.One of the highlights was the DIY batik painting area outside, where visitors can colour their own batik block art and bring it home. This is a fun and creative activity, perfect for families or school groups.Overall, Natural Batik Village is a mt-visit destination in Kuantan for anyone interested in Malaysian culture, traditional arts, or jt looking for a meaningful souvenir.

Pertama kali datang ke sini. Persekitaran cantik. Staff mesra dan sangat membantu. Cuma ada tiga benda yang saya rasa perlu penambahbaikan.1. No telefon yang diberikan di laman rasmi Facebook dan juga di Google Maps. Sebaiknya letaklah no telefon staff yang berada di lokasi sebenar. Saya hubungi no yang disediakan untuk membuat tempahan. Sebab kami sekeluarga datang pada cuti umum. Jawapan yang saya dapat, boleh datang walk in sahaja. Bila saya ajukan risau pondok yang saya mahu ditempah orang yang sampai lebih awal dari kami, jawapan dari owner, "tidak juga tu".Pada hari kejadian, saya call owner sebab tidak pasti akan lokasi, tapi tidak berjawab. Nasib baik ada no di papan tanda di hadapan simpang masuk untuk saya hubungi. Bila sampai di tempat pendaftaran, saya tunjukkan gambar pondok yang saya mahu, staff maklumkan, itu adalah gambar lama dan pondok baharu di tapak tersebut telah ditempah orang selama 2 malam.Syukur masih ada pondok bersebelahan yang kosong. Memandangkan saya membawa warga emas, jadi saya mencari lokasi yang tidak terlalu jauh untuk turun ke gigi air.2. Jalan masuk ke dalam rasanya perlu penambahbaikan. Ada satu bahagian jalan tidak rata menyukarkan kenderaan untuk masuk. Memandangkan permukaan jalan di bahagian tidak rata tersebut diletak batu-batu sederhana besar yang agak licin. Kenderaan masih boleh masuk cuma akan sedikit was-was memandangkan perjalanan untuk sampai ke campsite sedikit jauh masuk ke dalam. Risau jika berlaku kerosakan kepada kenderaan akan menyukarkan perjalanan balik. Memandangkan tidak semua pengunjung datang dari kawasan yang berdekatan.3. Kemudahan bilik mandi dan juga tandas. Bilik mandi sangat gelap dan pintu tidak dapat ditutup dari dalam. Perlu ada bantuan orang pegang dari luar. Lantai pula licin. Ini agak menyukar dan membahayakan apabila membawa warga emas. Selain itu kalau boleh untuk tambah jumlah bilik mandi sedia ada bagi menampung kapasiti pengunjung daytrip.Selebihnya, saya sangat puji staff yang bertugas. Sangat peramah dan membantu. Pondok yang kami sewa ditinggalkan oleh pengguna sebelum dalam keadaan kotor tetapi staff dengan pantas bersihkan dan tukar plastik sampah baharu. Saya request untuk tambahan keri pun dihantar dengan cepat. Tempat cantik, bersih dan keadaan sungai pun tidak terlalu dalam. Anak-anak dan parents pun enjoy. Highly recommended untuk datang bersantai di sini bersama keluarga.Saya sertakan gambar no telefon staff yang boleh dihubungi untuk buat tempahan.

We arrived at Notuhu Ecotourism around 4:30 PM for a brief visit and did not stay overnight. The entrance fee was RM6 per person, which is quite affordable. Parking was easy and conveniently located near the entrance.The highlight of the visit was the stunning view of Gunung Kinabalu. Although the peak was partially obscured by clouds when we arrived, the surrounding scenery was still breathtaking. The temperature was pleasantly cool, making it comfortable to plore the area. The river water was ceptionally clean, cool, and fresh, perfect for a quick dip or jt enjoying the natural ambiance.Facilities included well-maintained toilets and a small surau, which I ed. The surau was modest in size, resembling a small chalet, but it seed its purpose well. There is also a food stall on-site offering local snacks and meals, which added convenience for visitors.One downside is that the facilities provided for the RM6 entrance fee are quite basic, so I would rate the facilities themselves only 2 star. However, the natural beauty and peaceful environment more than make up for this.Internet connectivity was surprisingly good, allowing  to stay connected during our short visit.Overall, my first visit to Notuhu Ecotourism was a very positive perience. The combination of beautiful natural surroundings, cool climate, and clean river made it a refreshing escape. I look forward to returning for a longer stay to fully enjoy all the activities and the serene environment this ecotourism site has to offer.

Came here for some whitewater rafting but did not stay at the camp. Good website, responsive over email and lots of FAQs and guidance to inform you of the trip.Took my parents who are 60+ and had never been whitewater rafting before. We had a great time. Not the most challenging rapids but was still good fun with about 22 individual rapids in total.Depending on the river conditions it can take 30 minutes (heavy rains, fast flowing river) to 2hrs to complete the course with a 20min or so break in the middle for some drinking water and an apple. If the water is low it takes longer but is not as fun and the boat can get stuck. If the river is higher it's a lot rougher but over quicker! They advised the best time is around November to December if I recall for higher waters.Our guides were friendly and fun. They also provided lunch which was quite tasty back at the camp.The site is located in a small kampung and requires a lorry limo transfer (circa 30 mins) from the home base to the launch site. They provide lockers toilets and showers if neededWould love to come back to gopeng again for another round of rafting or perhaps some of their vario other activities in the future.The guide also shares photos via WhatsApp free of charge which is nice. We came here as a group of 23 to kayak and stand up paddle (SUP) down the kampar river. The compound itself is huge; comprising of a welcome area, canteen, and cabins. It's generally easy to park too. The guides were very professional; the briefings were clear and thorough. The river itself was very beautiful. The water was chilly, and the river cut deep into the forest - making you feel like you were far far from civilization. We travelled about 9km downriver (not much paddling required and the river was very calm) before getting picked up at the end and shuttled back to the starting point. Lunch was provided and the food was good too. Highly recommended. I'll definitely be back to do this again in a smaller party. I was under the impression (from a Grab driver’s recommendation) that this was going to be fried dough with peanut sauce, like a doughnut. NOT THAT. Truly bizarre food and perience—no regrets.Step one: get on line 1 and wait to take a very large plate and e tongs to select your choice of dozens of items from the counter.Step two: load you plate with a mountain of fish balls, lobster balls, deep fried tofu, deep fried cheese, battered whole lobsters, crabs, squids, octopes, giant prawns, shrimp cakes (all in their shells), cookies, and a variety of hot dogs and saages.Step three: get on line 2 and wait your turn to hand your mountain of fried wonders to the chef. He then chops it all up and tosses it like a salad with julienne cucumbers and does it a dark maroon nutty chili sauce.Step four: eat.A truly new culinary perience for me, truly positive perience— novel flavors and ttures, salty and sweet, crunchy and a little bit spicy. One was enough!$18myr for my portion, see pics. My plate was about a quarter the quantity of what most folks took. Was plenty for me, and to share.

If you’re in Penang, a walk down Armenian Street in George Town is an absolute mt for art enthiasts and culture seekers alike. The area is a vibrant blend of history and creativity, with its iconic street art installations perfectly complementing the heritage architecture.The murals, many of which were created by Lithuanian artist Ernest Zacharevic, are an essential part of Penang’s charm. From the playful “Little Children on a Bicycle” to the wistful “Boy on a Motorcycle,” these artworks are not jt paintings—they’re an interactive perience. Watching people pose creatively with the murals adds a delightful energy to the street.Beyond the murals, the area is alive with local binesses—boutique cafes, craft shops, and quaint galleries—adding depth to the cultural perience. It’s also worth ploring the hidden corners, as some of the lesser-known artworks and graffiti gems are tucked away in quiet alleys.Tip: Visit in the morning or late afternoon to avoid the midday heat. Comfortable shoes and a camera are essentials. If you’re here during the festive, visiting at night will have really nice light installations accompanying you too!Whether you’re here to soak in the art or enjoy the vibrant street life, Armenian Street truly encapsulates the soul of Penang. A stroll here leaves you inspired and deeply connected to the island’s cultural heartbeat.

Now I understand why a piece of traditional hand-weaved silk can cost as much as RM80k. Sayang. Yang perempuan, saya dapat tahu dari pembeli lain yang datang ambil barang, ada hati marah pembeli. Wah, kamu ingat kamu anak raja?Akhir kata, kerja biar ikhlas. Kamu mewakili kerajaan Pahang sebagai orang yang memasarkan tenunan Pahang, jadi silalah berbudi bahasa. Tak perlu bermesra terlebih pun, asalkan sopan dan berbudi bahasa.

We recently visited Rumah Makan Perpateh after a recommendation from the staff at Habeeb Carpet, and I mt say, we were pleasantly surprised!At first glance, the area seemed quiet, with several empty and closed shops around. For a moment, we thought the restaurant might not have many ctomers. However, once we stepped inside, it was a completely different story there were plenty of diners, which immediately reassured  that we were in for a good meal. No wonder there were so many parked cars outside!The ordering process was straightforward; we scanned the menu and browsed through the options. The restaurant specializes in traditional Malaysian dishes, and we found the prices to be quite reasonable. What impressed  most was the speed of seice the food arrived quickly, without compromising on quality.Now, onto the food itself absolutely delicio! The gulai ikan tenggiri was outstanding, perfectly balanced with jt the right amount of spice. It paired beautifully with sambal ikan bilis tempoyak, which added an tra layer of flavor. We also tried the sambal petai udang, and it was equally good. Every dish tasted authentic and well prepared, making it a truly satisfying dining perience.One suggestion for improvement perhaps adding more traditional vegetable dishes, especially ones that are harder to find in regular restaurants, would make the menu even more citing.Overall, we’re really glad we gave this place a try. The combination of great food, reasonable prices, and a warm dining atmosphere makes Rumah Makan Perpateh a mt-visit. Highly recommended!

24/12/2022, SabtuPengalaman yg menarik untuk dikongsi. Saya sangat kagum dgn idea penguran oleh pihak Ruza. Kreatif.Parkir kereta disediakan. Percuma.1. Pengunjung perlu beli token (macam hari kantin) bernilai minimum RM15 dan maksimum RM20.2. Dewasa perlu bayar token RM1 untuk menaiki tram ke tempat yang dinamakan Fasa 1. Untuk pulang, bayar token RM1. Jumlah token, RM2. Kanak² 2 tahun ke bawah percuma.3. Di Fasa 1, anda boleh mengambil gambar di tempat2 menarik dan pergi ke kedai² yang disediakan. Cuma, anda perlu bayar menggunakan token. Molla (surau) turut disediakan. Yg ni sgt dipuji sebab sgt kondif.  (Toilet No 2 tak boleh dipam)..4. Untuk masuk ke Fasa 2, pengunjung perlu bayar token RM5 per pax. Actually worth it..kawasan sgt luas. Bukan sekadar tempat bergambar. Ada juga pelbagai binatang seperti di Mini Zoo, sesuai utk kanak² kenali binatang seperti burung unta, landak dan sebagainya. Molla di Fasa 2 adalah dalam kontena berbentuk bas, sy prefer di Fasa 1.Overall, great job kepada pihak yg mengurkan. We need more of this. Fasa 3 (berdekatan parkir) akan dibuka Inshaallah bulan 2 Tahun 2023 semasa cuti Israk Mikraj. Fasa 4 juga tak lama selepas itu.

rm10 to enter only aqua themepark, rm10 to only enter zoo and rm15 for zoo with unta & photozone. full access rm25. but people with only 1/2 access can easily access other places becae no one’s incharge.im not writing this to corrupt anyone, but please make it better. first, the entrance was ok. shabby but after the buggy ride it was okay but still kinda unsafe by how the buggy looks. after we arrived at photozone, the place looks uh (not worth it) there’s only 1-3 photozone that looks “okay” to take pictures with and the other were so basic.there were rabbits full of blood stains and so dirty. from photozone u can rent a small buggy/bicycle. rm10-25 for only AN hour. the place to places are so far and they pect to charge that much.we cant help but rent it becae our journey to mini zoo was so long it’s so far (buggy is so shabby and waiting for its time to come) the mini zoo was so disappointing. animals were locked in, caged, dirty and looking depressed. u want to enter cat hoe? pay rm10. you want to come inside and play a small playground? pay rm10. everything starts at rm10???after i sent my fam to the aqua side, i need to return the buggy since i dont want it to be overdue and waited for another free buggy to go back to aqua side.aqua side was FULL OF FLIES 😭 the foodie side was so so so dirty. and the food were so pensive. bihun soup tasted good but the portion was like a kid’s meal. nasi was jt ok. air gas rm4, maggie cup for rm5 and if u want them to cook it for you, pay more. cabana (a place to sit) at aquaside started from rm20+ even a pondok is rm20, u even need to pay to sit at a very small pondok. the pool was ok looking ok but can u imagine how the parents feel with the surrounding? they said rented buggy cant go past aqua side but there’s literally people who easily ride there and take pictures with buggy. if want it to be strictly paid, then be systematic. its not fair for people who paid for full access. nothing soecial about it!staff were nice. no complaints but the place is jt, sorry to say, so bad. i would not come again to this place. i think if u love unorganised kampung styled zoo and you’re rich RICH. this is a place for u. i could spend 1k if i pay for everything with family here. i feel like the only free thing is a toilet. so disappointed. i thought by paying rm25 i can get real full access…. but it jt a different waistband. no one cares about it. what a waste of land. u guys should open fasa 1-2 and make it BETTER before u open until fasa 6. pondok should be free. food should be less pensive.bad perience but here’s a good memory i got from here. 1-the moment when i ride a rent shabby buggy for an hour with my fam becae my brother is a funny tour guide. 2- a staff asking another staff to stop working so we can see the horses (who looks depressed🙂‍↕️) the staff was so nice he smiled and take care of his ctomers so i can take it to my heart.that much of check points but i only have 4 photos of things there. a horse, running duck, an empty road and me riding buggy. there’s no even ootd worthy spot hahaha. overall jt bad, im gaslighting myself but i failed.

🍍MD2 Pineapple Plantation Visit – Negeri SembilanSweet pineapples, yes—but it was the interns who left the deepest impression.This MD2 pineapple plantation in Negeri Sembilan produces ceptionally sweet fruit with the signature low acidity of the variety. But what truly made this visit memorable was meeting the group of dedicated young interns running much of the operation behind the scenes.They came from across Malaysia—Sabah, Pahang, and beyond—bringing not jt geographical diversity, but a shared spirit of commitment. Many are students pursuing agriculture-related studies, and their decision to intern far from home speaks volumes about their drive.Despite the tropical heat and long hours, they were energetic, articulate, and genero with their knowledge. From plaining soil prep to walking  through the science of pineapple enzymes, they handled it all with the kind of clarity and professionalism you might pect from seasoned guides.One of them even introduced me to gac fruit, a strikingly red superfruit grown onsite, sharing its nutritional benefits and haesting process with quiet enthiasm.In short, this was not jt a plantation visit—it was a masterclass in what happens when youth, learning, and land come together.Highly recommended for anyone keen to witness Malaysia’s nt generation of agri-leaders in action—oh, and yes, the pineapples are absolutely worth the trip.

We made a rather spontaneo dash to Kuala Selangor one rainy evening—departed KL around 7pm and reached jt an hour later, umbrellas at the ready and spirits still high. The goal? To catch a glimpse of the famed fireflies, one of Kuala Selangor’s most magical nighttime spectacles.A word to the wise: the best time to visit is between 7 and 8pm. Any later and you risk missing the show, as the fireflies start to dim their glow and call it a night. Timing, it seems, is everything when dealing with nature’s own fairy lights.Now, if you’re lucky and time your trip jt right, you might also witness the enchanting blue algae phenomenon—where the water lights up in a surreal neon blue glow. It’s like the sea threw a rave and forgot to invite the humans. But do note, this only happens during high tide and certain months, with June being the most likely candidate.Each perience—fireflies and blue algae—has its own dedicated boat tour, and we went with Jet Sky Mirror Kuala Selangor, a name that’s built a solid reputation in these parts. The boats are well-covered (a blessing in unpredictable weather), and the crew clearly prioritise safety and know-how, which is always reassuring when you’re out on the river after dark.Tours are reasonably priced at RM35 for adults and RM30 for children (local rates), though foreign visitors can pect a slightly higher fare. The ride lasts about an hour—jt long enough to soak up the serenity and take a few “ooh and ahh” photos without overdoing it.All in all, it’s an perience that blends nature, wonder, and a touch of adventure. Jt check the tides, keep an eye on the clock, and let Kuala Selangor do the rest.

Sungai Lima, nestled on the quieter side of Pulau Ketam, offers a refreshing escape from the btling main village. Unlike its more commercialized counterpart, Sungai Lima embraces a laid-back, rtic charm that immediately puts visitors at ease. The moment you step off the ferry, you're greeted by a slower pace of life, where the rhythm of the tides dictates daily activities.The primary draw here is undoubtedly the tranquility and the immersive perience of a traditional fishing village. Stilt hoes, painted in vario shades and adorned with drying nets, line the waterways, creating picturesque scenes perfect for photography. You can obsee local fishermen mending their nets, children playing on makeshift jetties, and the occasional boat ferrying goods – a genuine slice of local life.Dining options are simpler but authentic. Fresh seafood, caught jt hours before, is the star of most men. Don't pect fancy restaurants; instead, anticipate homely eateries seing up delicio, unpretentio dishes. While there aren't many organized activities, the joy lies in wandering the narrow wooden walkways, soaking in the peaceful atmosphere, and interacting with the friendly locals.Accommodation is basic, often guesthoes run by families, providing a no-frills but comfortable stay. It’s ideal for those seeking a quiet retreat, a digital detox, and a chance to perience the unvarnished beauty of Pulau Ketam. Sungai Lima is a gem for travelers who appreciate authenticity over tourist traps, offering a genuine glimpse into Malaysian coastal village life.

An art gallery disguised as a souvenir shop: intricate jewellery, hand-painted ceramics, delicate ttiles, and postcards so beautiful you’ll want to send one to yourself. The location is perfect, tucked near Gaya Street, with a post office nearby so you can send those gorgeo postcards on the spot.On paper, it’s brilliant - local artists and handmade souvenirs. But it’s not really for the locals. And the price tags reflect that. Sabahans might stop by, admire the work, but chances are they’ll walk out empty-handed. Even some tourists feel the sting. A budget-friendly section wouldn’t hurt. Stickers, small prints, little things that don’t make your wallet cry. You see them in art markets, so it’s not impossible.The staff are friendly and fluent in English and Chinese, which makes life easier for tourists. No phy sales tactics, jt quiet smiles. But it’d be great if they knew more about the artists - who they are, what makes them special. Even a simple write-up could make all the difference. At a glance, the pieces have that polished, mass-market “Malaysia” look - bright, colourful, and safe. With a little tweaking, this place could be a true cultural landmark rather than jt a pretty (and pricey) shop.

My son wanted to perience the trending glamping stay.I booked a night stay here for RM210.00, which is a couple tent. My son brought his own sleeping bag to sleep in.There are 3 types of tents : 2 unit of each family, couple and single tents. And priced at RM250, RM210 and RM120 respectively with added surcharge of RM30 on weekend, school and public holidays.I was advised by the opeartor to park within the compound of Ad Diniah school. As soon as I arrived and parked my car, I inquired about the parking payment and a school teacher told me to leave and not park my car there. I asked the Glamping operator and he told me to park at the roadside. It was really irresponsible of them to jt brh aside security and safety problems of the car park. The roadside is pretty narrow and so, I decided to leave my car in the school compound.To reach the glamping site, there is a 30m hill hike with hand rails to assist.The glamping site also offers a DIY Steamboat set with added cost on seice and rental of fire pit.Once there, we were told of the only toilet to e and some tips on staying in the tent. We had the farthest tent at the site and quite a walk to the toilet. The toilet was smelly and totally inadequate. We had to share that one toilet with the other tent dwellers. It is really disgting.There is not much of activities to do here. The terrain in beautiful. I was hoping to do some hiking and some high or low rope activities here. But none is available here. It would have been great, though. None of the dwellers took the DIY steamboat, either.And early the nt morning at about 8.30am, the  opeartor came by the tent and cleared the rubbish and waited to clean it. We actually had until 12 noon to check out. But, I felt like we were being chased away. We quickly packed our things and left.I find that glamping is not for me, especially on the issue of dirty and distant toilet. The absence of activities at the glamping site is also another frtrating issue.

Honest review:✅ ‘Masak Lemak Daging Salai’ 5/5✅ Dish Variety 5/5✅ Staff friendliness 4/5✅ Eating space 4/5✅ Cleanliness 5/5✅ Parking 5/5‘Masak Lemak Daging Salai’ enthiasts, this is the right place to give it a go. As for me, my friends brought me here to taste for the first time, I came back on my own the nt day for lunch.The gravy is cooked to my liking. The ‘lemak’ from the coconut milk and the level of spiciness is well done. I even drank the ‘kuah’ from the bowl on my first time there. Loved the chilli kick!They also seed several other dishes. And for ‘masak lemak’, they have vario proteins such as duck, chicken, free-range chicken ‘ayam kampung’, quail, mhroom and white lipped-eel catfish ‘ikan sembilang’.As for the desserts, you can enjoy ‘cendol’ and ‘tapai’ (made from fermented rice).I highly recommend this place. And for sure I will revisit this place in future.

I rarely leave reviews, but this one is well deseed. I visited their Senawang branch recently and was genuinely impressed from start to finish. The food was absolutely delicio, especially the grilled fish. Not even the slightest hint of fishy smell, which is so rare! It was cooked to perfection and full of flavor.The staff were also incredibly friendly and sincere. After our meal, they checked in to make sure everything was alright, and it didn’t feel scripted at all. You could really feel their genuine care and hospitality.Later, after leaving and already on the highway, we realized we forgot to inform the counter that we added tra rice ourselves. I called (I believe it was the Ulu Bendul branch that picked up) to let them know and asked if I could transfer any additional charges. But the lady who answered was so kind and soft spoken, and simply said “it’s okay, halal je.” Honestly I was touched. It’s rare to find a restaurant with such heart, honesty, and genuine seice.they left a beautiful impression on me. Great food, great people, and a warm perience overall. Highly recommended!

We had been struggling if we should visit this place not sure what to pect. Eventually the temptations were jt too great to resist looking at photos from other travellers. Coming from an urban city like Singapore of course poses some challenges but our hosts made sure we are comfortable and nicely settled in. The home is rtic and simple, but definitely very functional. The opportunity to sample a lot of local delicio kampong meals whipped up by Maureen is one of the highlights.   We felt so pampered and this could go down as one of the most enjoyable holiday ever.   There are several dogs and a cat at the hoe and if you love these furry friends, you will be in for a good time, they are so adorable.  We had so much fun together and they would follow  wherever we go. Seeing them playfully running in the padi field is such a joy.  By the way Andy, thank you for this wonderful itinerary, it was so enjoyable and memories of our stay here will linger on for a long time. Linah, Anne and Chia.

We were only here for three days, which isn't really enough, especially for an outdoor enthiast who enjoys trekking and nature. Internet access is limited, and the only free Wi-Fi is at the Barjo town telecenter, which is about 2 km from the hoe.

Thank you so much, Scott, for a wonderful stay at Barjo! Very late to the party as we were there in September 2016, but if there's one place to stay while visiting Barjo, this place hits the nail on the head! Scott and his family have an incredible history in the Kelabit Highlands and absolutely spoiled  with their hospitality! We visited Barjo when they had a huge school fire that swept through the actual school building not far from Ngimat Ayu. It was incredible to see how a very tiny community came together to help in a very real and terrifying situation! We really didn't miss it! A home away from home! There's plenty to do in Barjo: hiking, fishing, cycling, or losing yourself in a good book! Great little local pub/shop too :) Getting to know the locals is fantastic! We would love to come back someday. Thank you so much!

We celebrated Christmas and hered in the New Year here with plenty of food and merrymaking. Bicycles are available, as is transportation in Barjo. Bed sizes vary from twin beds to kings. There's plenty to do around Barjo (especially ploring the surrounding nature), jt ask your hosts!

A trip to Barjo takes you far, far away from the tourist crowds, into the city jungle. The Twin Otter flight from Miri itself is an perience. If you can't fly, you can always find someone to give you a lift for a 12-hour drive (if the bridges haven't been washed out). We spent our short stay at the Ngimat Ayu B&B at Buad Main Beduh. Our hosts kept a clean place, and we ate what they ate. The amenities are quite basic, so don't pect 5 stars, but you will enjoy the warm welcome and ceptional hospitality.

A wonderful retreat in the Kelabit Highlands. Scott greeted  at the airport and took  on a short scenic drive to Ngimat Ayu Hoe. The charming hoe sat on a hillside with a beautiful view of the PADI fields.

This is actly how we felt when we stayed at this beautiful B&B. You can feel the love in every corner of Ayu Ngimat's home. We perienced the incredible hospitality of Scott and his charming and beautiful mother (Tepu) and Nigel. The kitchen was always full of wonderful aromas, and the sunsets from the elevated veranda were surreal. Scott and Nigel took the time to chat with  while we were enjoying the peaceful surroundings during the current Pesta Nukenen. We cycled around the village and hiked up Prayer Mountain easily from the private accommodation. Thank you so much for an unforgettable trip! xxx

A very comfortable stay in Barjo, good food in a home-style kitchen with an attached restaurant featuring a warm fireplace. The food in Kelabit was very tasty: barking deer soup, yellow round fried dough, sweet Barjo pineapple, Sultan fish, Barjo rice, midin... new and interesting flavors.

cellent place to stay and relax. The balcony is so comfortable that I was very relieved to leave the hoe. cellent hosts, especially Aunt Martha. She is so motherly and cooks very well. I would definitely stay again for a long, long time... By the way, the hotel is very clean. I highly recommend it.

The area around Bario can be plored for 6 days, which Apoi/Scott, the owner of Ngimat Ayu, organized for  (itinerary included in the price). A 3-day trek from Sarawak to Kalimantan and back to Sarawak was also included.

We received a warm welcome from our group of six. After freshening up, we were shown to the barbecue area for hot snacks, including wild boar (delicio). Our rooms were comfortable, and the bathroom facilities, although shared, were adequate and clean. The hoe itself is a beautiful longhoe with a huge porch where you can spend your time relaxing and enjoying the view. The meals were very good; we were seed a good selection of dishes, all locally sourced. Some things we had never eaten before were quisite. Staying here for two nights gave  the opportunity to plore the Bario area and visit some of the local villages. Scott and his mother were very friendly and accommodating. If I were to come back to Bario, I would definitely stay here again.

Wonderful hospitality. I was warmly greeted by Apoi at the airport and went to meet her at her home in Tepu (my grandmother's hoe in Kelabit). Every meal was seed with ceptional local cuisine, and there were also some local snacks for tea. The veranda overlooked the PADI fields and mountains, where I could relax with a cup of tea and a drink. I would definitely return.
Absolutely perfect. Very welcoming, warm, clean, and the food is great. The bathing water quality is 5 stars! :)

I was truly delighted with this B&B. The layout was warm and inviting. The beds were comfortable. Scott, the owner, is a fantastic cook. Smoked wild boar, venison, and jungle vegetables on a cold night... delicio! This place is high up on the ground floor overlooking rice paddies. There are shared bathrooms and toilets. No internet in this part of Borneo, but who cares? Plenty of fresh air, quiet time to unwind, climb Prayer Mountain and learn how to plant or haest rice... depending on the month you're there. Electricity comes on from about 5:00 PM to 11:00 PM. You're behind bars, but the idea flows! Love the simplicity of village life. Perfect if you really need to de-stress!

We stayed here for three nights in early July 2015. Scot and Jenni were incredibly friendly, reflecting the incredible warmth and hospitality of the Kelabit Highlands people. We were a group of six, three of whom were vegetarians. Scot and Jenni went out of their way to ensure we were well looked after. We ate wonderfully and slept well. The balcony was an cellent place to relax, read a book, and watch the Highlands go by... it felt like home. As long as you're not looking for the Lamin Hilton and your heart is set on a genuine perience with nice people, you'll be well placed to stay here.

We only stayed for one night, but we would have loved to stay longer. We received a warm welcome and felt very comfortable. Ngimat Ayu Hoe is lovely, with several areas for relaxation. The food was plentiful and delicio, always offering great new dishes, especially the local cuisine.

I've jt returned from this dream trip and am now back in KL. It was an incredible soul-searching journey through a breathtaking landscape. There's nothing better than a hotel here becae it isn't. It's a home. Apoi and her career meant my mother treated me like a family member, not a guest, during my stay. She shares her life honestly and with vigor. I never felt completely idle in Bario. Remote control as well as it may be, you'll feel there's so much more. cellent for trekking.
